summary.brief: Hernandez v. Barthold. Medical malpractice case involving emergency medicine.

evidence_index.text: Hernandez alleged that the defendants failed to adequately assess him for appendicitis, hospitalize him or seek a surgical consultation prior to discharge, properly interpret the testing ordered by the previous emergency room physician, and give adequate discharge instructions.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 17, 2011, Hernandez presented to the emergency room with complaints of abdominal pain that began three weeks prior as sharp pain located 'in the left upper quadrant.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Hernandez was originally seen by staff physician Lamberty and the resident working with him, Dr. Bhargava Mullapudi.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: An abdominal CT scan was ordered, which was initially read by resident radiologist Dr. Joel Brink.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Hernandez’ white blood cell count was 13,700; the medical record shows that the normal range is 4,000 to 11,000.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: When Lamberty’s shift ended, Barthold took over as Hernandez’s attending physician.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Barthold diagnosed Hernandez with epigastric pain, dyspepsia and abdominal pain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Hernandez returned to the emergency room on March 19, 2011, with a ruptured appendix.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Schurgin also opined that if the standard of care had been met, Hernandez would have most likely undergone a laparoscopic appendectomy.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Schurgin

evidence_index.text: In contrast, Barthold’s expert, Cunningham, opined that Barthold met the standard of care.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Cunningham

evidence_index.text: The jury returned a general verdict in favor of Barthold.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We conclude that the district court did not err with regard to the instructions given to the jury or in its evidentiary ruling during cross-examination of Barthold.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented with abdominal pain that began three weeks prior as sharp pain in the left upper quadrant, with nausea and vomiting after eating tacos. By March 17, the pain was new on the right side near the belt area and was severe.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Abdominal pain with suspicion of appendicitis

requestables.name: CT Abdomen
requestables.description: CT scan of the abdomen to evaluate for appendicitis

reveal.result_summary: Appendix slightly enlarged at 1.1 cm, no wall thickening or inflammatory changes
reveal.clinical_significance: May be normal variant, but requires close follow-up in context of abdominal pain
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt consideration of appendicitis and surgical consult

requestables.name: White Blood Cell Count
requestables.description: Complete blood count to assess for infection

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with abdominal pain

key_events.event: Patient triaged and initial assessment performed

timeline_phases.name: Diagnostic Workup
timeline_phases.description: CT scan and lab tests performed

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with severe abdominal pain and elevated WBC
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.description: Admit patient for observation and surgical consult
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care for suspected appendicitis with elevated WBC and CT findings

options.description: Discharge patient with instructions to return if symptoms worsen
options.clinical_reasoning: Inadequate given the suspicion of appendicitis

actual_action_defendant.description: Patient was discharged with instructions to return if symptoms worsened
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant relied on initial CT interpretation and patient's reported improvement

expected_action_court.description: Patient should have been admitted for observation and surgical consult
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated this was the standard of care

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to admit and consult surgery given clinical presentation and test results
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Patient should have been admitted for further evaluation
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient returned with ruptured appendix requiring emergency surgery
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Increased scarring and recovery time due to open surgery

key_findings: Jury found no negligence by Barthold
Standard of care was met according to defense expert

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to admit and consult surgery
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Admitting patient could have prevented rupture

factual_timeline.event: Patient presents to ED with abdominal pain
Patient returns to ED with ruptured appendix

tests_performed.result: Appendix slightly enlarged, no wall thickening

allegations.allegation: Failure to adequately assess and manage suspected appendicitis

court_findings.legal_significance: Jury verdict in favor of Barthold
